tldr: Benchmarks measure what is easy to measure, not what matters — and the gap kills production systems.
core_argument: Synthetic benchmarks are systematically misleading because they cannot capture the emergent behavior of real production systems under realistic load, contention, and hardware interactions.

premises: Benchmarks measure isolated, warm-cache, single-workload scenarios.
Production systems face mixed workloads, cold starts, and cross-NUMA contention.
Instrumentation perturbs the system being measured (the Heisenbug analogue).
inferences: A benchmark that misleads once undermines trust in all future measurements.
Mean throughput hides tail latency, which is what matters at scale.
argument_graph.conclusion: Treat every benchmark as a hypothesis; your production system is the experiment.

key_claims.claim: Benchmarks measure what is easy to measure, not what matters.
key_claims.evidence_quote: benchmarks measure what is easy to measure, not what matters
key_claims.evidence_context: Opening framing — sets up the core critique.

key_claims.claim: A kernel patch showed 40% throughput improvement in synthetic tests but doubled p99 latency in production.
key_claims.evidence_quote: Their team ran a synthetic benchmark that showed a 40% throughput improvement after a kernel patch
key_claims.evidence_context: Case study from a major cloud provider in 2019.

key_claims.claim: Trust is hard to build and easy to destroy — the same applies to benchmarks.
key_claims.evidence_quote: Trust is hard to build and easy to destroy
key_claims.evidence_context: Analogy from interpersonal trust to measurement credibility.

memorable_examples.title: Cloud provider kernel patch disaster
memorable_examples.story: A synthetic benchmark showed 40% throughput gain. Production p99 doubled because of cross-NUMA memory access patterns invisible to the isolated test.
memorable_examples.lesson: Workload isolation in benchmarks conceals hardware interactions that dominate in production.

notable_quotes: The best way to predict the future is to invent it. — Alan Kay
Measure more, trust less. Every benchmark is a hypothesis. Your production system is the experiment.
steelman: Benchmarks ARE useful as regression detectors within a controlled, reproducible environment — the problem is treating their outputs as production truth rather than directional signals.
strongest_rebuttal: Without any benchmarking discipline, engineering teams fly blind. A misleading benchmark is better than no benchmark at all.
would_change_mind_if: Evidence that teams using synthetic benchmarks exclusively ship fewer production regressions than teams using production shadow traffic.
in_conversation_with: Jeff Dean and Luiz Barroso — 'The Tail at Scale' (CACM 2013)
article: Benchmarks are seductive. The gap between measurement and reality kills production systems.
